title: Tensor ops SIGSEGV on non-tensor (vector) input instead of raising a type error
goal: DEEP finding. Tensor activation/ops codegen assumes a HEAP_SUBTYPE_TENSOR input and does NOT validate the type; given a heterogeneous vector (HEAP_SUBTYPE_VECTOR) it misreads the memory layout and SIGSEGVs instead of raising a type error. Confirmed: (softmax (reshape (vector 0.0 0.0 0.0 0.0) 4)) => #(0.25 0.25 0.25 0.25) OK; (softmax (vector 1.0 2.0 3.0)) => SIGSEGV. A crash on a type mismatch is never acceptable (it is also a robustness/DoS issue for any program that passes the wrong shape). Likely shared across the tensor_activation_codegen / tensor_*_codegen ops (softmax, gelu, sigmoid, etc.) which all unpack the tensor struct without a subtype guard.
next_actions: Add a tensor-subtype guard at the entry of tensor ops: if the operand is not HEAP_SUBTYPE_TENSOR, either coerce a homogeneous numeric vector to a tensor, or raise a clean type error (eshkol_type_error_with_operand) — never proceed to unpack a non-tensor struct.
Audit all tensor ops (activations softmax/gelu/sigmoid/silu/elu/selu, reduce, conv, pool, matmul, transpose, norms) for the missing guard; centralize the unpack+validate helper.
Add tests/ml/tensor_type_guard_test.esk: every tensor op given a vector/int/string raises a catchable type error (no segfault); valid tensor inputs unaffected.
acceptance: (softmax (vector 1.0 2.0 3.0)) raises a catchable type error (or coerces), does NOT segfault
all tensor ops reject non-tensor inputs with a type error rather than crashing
valid tensor inputs still produce correct results; AOT + -r
campaign_payoff: Removes a whole class of segfaults (tensor op + wrong input type) — robustness + safety; a type error a program can catch instead of a hard crash.
status_note: PR #79 — centralized tensor operand guard; 49/49 -r+AOT (+wasm stub)
